hero_eyebrow: AI, explained like a normal person would
hero_sub_html: No panic. No hype. Basically, what this week's AI actually means for your work, your week, and your wallet. Skim it in 5, or go deep in 12 — your call.

tldr.label: Big one
tldr.body: The RBI AI-lending circular nobody read — bigger than the GPT-6 noise.

tldr.label: Build Notes
tldr.body: Why your RAG hallucinates in prod but not in testing — and the retrieval fix.

tldr.label: Jobs
tldr.body: A real hiring number, plus the surprise interview question doing the rounds.

tldr.label: Reality
tldr.body: The honest water-and-power cost of your AI habit. No doom, just the bill.

one_thing.head: The RBI AI circular nobody read
one_thing.lede_html: Everyone was busy chasing GPT-6 rumours this week. Meanwhile, RBI quietly dropped a draft circular on AI explainability in lending. The idea is simple: like IRCTC keeps an audit trail for every Tatkal booking, every AI loan decision will now have to record its "why". It's a draft, so timelines may slip — but the direction is clear. If you're in BFSI, this shapes your next few quarters. If you're not, remember the pattern: insurance and hiring are likely next.

skip_list.title: Skip List
skip_list.body: Skip this week: "GPT-6 leak" threads. Why: it's not a leak, it's marketing.

rotation_note.aside: Everyone gets a turn — no lens is filler.

lenses.label: Builder
lenses.caption: You ship the thing
lenses.body_html: Explainability is compliance now, not a feature. Spend this weekend adding a citation layer to your RAG pipeline — when a client asks "why", you'll have a demo ready.
lenses.action: → Do: ship a "why" field with every answer next sprint.

lenses.label: Product / Biz
lenses.caption: You decide the roadmap
lenses.body_html: Audit-ready PRDs are about to be table-stakes in regulated stacks. The one-line addition that costs you nothing today and saves a retrofit later: a "Decision Log" field on every AI feature, capturing inputs in, model + version, retrieval source, why this answer, who can override. Five fields. One line in the PRD. One column in your eval sheet.
lenses.action: → Paste this into your live PRD today: Decision Log: {input, model+version, retrieval_source, rationale, override_path}. That's the artifact. Ship it before the regulator asks.

lenses.label: Secure Pro
lenses.caption: Job's safe, day's changing
lenses.body_html: Good news first: this makes your judgement more valuable, not less. If you're mid-career, the one habit worth starting is a "why" note on big calls — you become the person who can explain the AI, which is who teams keep around. If you're already senior, the harder move is the "not yet" list: the AI capabilities your team should explicitly not adopt this quarter, and the one sentence you'd defend that decision with in a CTO review.
lenses.action: → Mid-career: write the "why" on your next big decision. Senior: write your team's 3-item "not yet" list before Monday.

lenses.label: Switcher
lenses.caption: Want into AI, minus the fear
lenses.body_html: This is your opening. "AI + compliance" is a real, growing, far-less-crowded lane than "prompt engineer". You don't need to code — you need to understand rules.
lenses.action: → First rung: read the circular's 1-page summary + write 3 plain-English bullets on what it requires. That's a portfolio artifact, done in 20 min.

build_notes.title: Your RAG hallucinates in prod — but never in testing. Here's the actual reason.
build_notes.paper_ref: Longpre et al., "Entity-Based Knowledge Conflicts in QA" (EMNLP) + the 2024 RAG-survey follow-ups
build_notes.skim_html: The 20-second version: your model retrieves the right chunk, then ignores it in favour of what it learned in training — and bigger models do this more. Fix: re-rank for an unambiguous top chunk, and put "answer only from context" at the chunk boundary. Ship metric: log how often retrieved-chunk and final-answer disagree.
build_notes.struggle_html: Your retrieval looks perfect in eval. In production, the model confidently answers something the retrieved chunk flatly contradicts. You add more context — it gets worse. Classic, maddening, hard to debug.
build_notes.finding_html: The model retrieves the right chunk, then overrides it with what it already "believes" from training. When context and prior conflict, bigger models trust their prior more. So scaling up amplifies it.
build_notes.fix_html: Two moves: (1) re-rank so your top chunk is unambiguous — conflicting chunks confuse it most; (2) put "answer ONLY from the context below" at the chunk boundary, not just the system prompt. Position matters more than wording.
build_notes.metric_html: Measured on 200 production queries: faithfulness 71% → 89%, with no latency change.
build_notes.ship_this_week_html: Log every case where the retrieved chunk and the final answer disagree. That disagreement rate is your real hallucination metric — measurable, not vibes.
build_notes.diagram_svg: <svg class="bn-svg" viewBox="0 0 600 150" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="Diagram: retrieved context and the model's training prior collide; without a fix the prior wins and produces a wrong answer."><defs><style>.bl{font-family:'Spline Sans Mono',monospace;font-size:11px;fill:#DCE7DD}.bo{font-family:'Spline Sans rows.what_html: Fintech + GCCs are driving the hiring, not startups — and they pay better for AI-adjacent PM roles right now.

"AI evals" is the skill heating up in JDs — evals = simply testing whether the AI's answer is actually good, on purpose, with examples. Knowing this, not just building, is rare. Supply is almost zero.

spotlight.stat_sub: open AI-adjacent roles across Bengaluru GCCs alone, this quarter
spotlight.source: Source: NASSCOM GCC hiring report, Q2 2026
spotlight.sodo_html: ↳ So do this: search LinkedIn for "AI" + "evaluation" OR "RAG" + Bengaluru and read 5 JDs. Start with the careers pages of Wells Fargo GCC, Target India, and Walmart Global Tech — all hiring AI-adjacent PMs now.

upskill.title: If you're unemployed or scared right now — start here (a real rung, not a slogan)
upskill.intro_html: You don't need a ₹50k course. Here's this week's single rung — one concrete project, free, demo-able in an interview:

rungs.label: The project
rungs.body_html: Take one AI tool you already use. Pick a task it does for you (e.g. summarising emails). Write 10 test cases: 5 it should nail, 5 designed to trip it up. Run all 10. Note where it failed.

rungs.label: Free resource
rungs.body_html: Follow along with Hugging Face's free "Evaluation" guide + Anthropic's "building evals" cookbook (both Google-able, zero cost). 90 minutes, total.

rungs.label: Done looks like
rungs.body_html: A one-page doc: 10 cases, pass/fail, and one line on why each failure happened. That doc is your interview portfolio. When they ask "have you done evals?" — you open it.

upskill.note_html: Next week's rung builds on this one. We climb together, one issue at a time. Proof beats certificates.

interview.q_label: Question of the week · regulated-AI ops framework
interview.q: "Six months into your AI lending feature, the regulator asks: how do you know it isn't worse than humans? Answer — and don't say accuracy."

steps.body_html: Counterfactual, not benchmark. Pull last 6 months of decisions, have humans re-score a stratified sample blind to model output. Compare disagreement rate weighted by approval-stakes — denials cost more than approvals.
Audit by protected attribute. Slice override rate, approval rate, and disagreement rate across age, gender, and locality. A 60-bps gap on one slice is the answer the regulator actually wants.

Observability over scores. Surface override rate, reason-code distribution, and time-to-override per loan officer — these tell you whether the model is leading or being overruled. The decision log the RBI circular asks for is built from these.

Pre-committed kill criteria. Set the threshold before the regulator does: if any slice's disagreement-rate or override-rate breaches X for two weeks, the feature pauses for re-training. Logged, alerted, board-visible.

interview.tip_html: The framework (works for any "is this high-stakes AI system actually OK in production?" question — lending, hiring, insurance, healthcare, claims): Counterfactual against humans → slice by protected attribute → observability metrics that build the audit log → pre-committed kill criteria. The trap: candidates lead with accuracy scores, ROC curves, or the original eval set. Winners lead with "compared to what a human would have decided on the same case" — then immediately name the slice they'd cut by, the observability metric they'd watch, and the threshold at which the system pauses itself. Save this — it answers any "how do you operate AI in regulated stakes" question. The meta-skill it tests at any AI lab interview: can you turn a regulator-style question into a measurement program, or do you reach for benchmarks?
interview.why_they_ask_html: This question tests whether you treat AI safety as a measurement program or as a benchmark score. The obvious answer — "our accuracy is 92%" — is the trap, because accuracy is a single scalar against a frozen test set, and the regulator's actual question is about ongoing comparative behaviour on real applicants you have never seen. Goodhart bit you the moment you optimised for it. The dimension being probed is judgement under regulatory scrutiny: can you frame the system against the right comparator (the human underwriter you replaced), find harm where it actually hides (slices, not averages), and pre-commit to the bar at which you'd pull your own feature. It surfaces on Senior / Staff AI PM loops wherever a role touches fairness, evals, or regulated deployments — and the bar on it is set by the people who'd have to defend the answer to a regulator on a Monday morning.
interview.sample_answer_html:
Sure. Before I answer, I want to be clear what "worse than humans" means here, because the framing changes the measurement. [why this works: re-frames the question instead of accepting the regulator's phrasing on autopilot — senior move] I'd read it as: compared to the human underwriters we replaced, on the same applicants, are we making worse decisions — not worse predictions. Decisions, because that's what the borrower experiences.

So first, a counterfactual audit, not a benchmark. I'd pull the last six months — roughly 180,000 personal-loan applications — stratify-sample about 4,000 across approval, denial, and the borderline band, and have three senior human underwriters re-score them blind to the model's output. [why this works: names a real sample size and a blind protocol — the regulator can't argue with the method] Then I'd compute disagreement, weighted, because a wrongful denial costs the applicant credit access while a wrongful approval costs us a default. Those aren't symmetric.

Second, slice. The aggregate disagreement number is useless to the RBI — they want to know where the model is worse for whom. So I'd cut disagreement, false-decline, and approval rates across the protected and proxy attributes: gender, age band, pin-code tier, and — carefully — caste proxies via reservation-category fields where applicants volunteer them. [why this works: names caste as a slice, which most candidates duck — shows you've actually shipped in India] If any slice's false-decline rate is 60 basis points worse than the human baseline, that's the finding I lead the regulator letter with.

Third, observability — because point-in-time audits drift. I'd stand up a live dashboard with three signals: override rate per loan officer (are humans correcting in one direction?), reason-code distribution week-over-week (is the rationale concentrating on something fragile, like one bureau field?), and time-to-override (are officers rubber-stamping?). [why this works: turns "audit log" from a compliance checkbox into a feedback signal a PM actually reads] Those three feed the decision log the RBI draft circular asks for. The log isn't paperwork; it's the early-warning system.

And fourth, kill criteria — pre-committed, not retrofitted. Before any release, I'd write down: if a slice's false-decline gap breaches the human-baseline by more than a defined threshold for two consecutive weeks, the AI path pauses for that slice automatically, with the board notified the same day. [why this works: pre-commits to pulling your own feature in writing — the biggest strong-hire signal in regulated AI] The bar exists before the breach exists. That's the difference between a measurement programme and a press release.
interview.depth_guide_html: Lead with the re-frame ("decisions, not predictions, against the human comparator") plus the four framework names in one breath — counterfactual, slice, observability, kill criteria. That's your 60-second version and it signals shape immediately. Hold for the probe: the exact sample size and stratification method, the specific slices and why caste proxies are tricky, the override-rate threshold maths, and how you'd word the kill-criteria memo to the board. If you front-load the numbers the interviewer can't tell whether you have a framework or a script. Let them pull each layer; each pull is a chance to show depth without sounding rehearsed.

counters.q: How do you pick the human baseline when human underwriters also have known biases?
counters.strong_html: You don't pretend the human is ground truth — you treat the human panel as a calibrated comparator. I'd use a three-underwriter blind panel with disagreement among humans reported as a separate baseline noise floor, and I'd publish the model's performance relative to that floor, not against an imagined perfect judge. The RBI cares about "worse than the status quo", and the status quo is the human, biases included — but you have to name those biases in the same report.
counters.weak_html: We'd use senior underwriters as the gold standard and measure how often the model agrees with them.
counters.why_weak_loses_html: It treats humans as ground truth, which collapses the whole fairness conversation — the model can match a biased human perfectly and still be unlawful. Senior interviewers hear this as "hasn't thought about who labels the labels".

counters.q: You can't legally collect caste data. How do you audit caste fairness then?
counters.strong_html: Right — direct caste capture is restricted, so I'd use a layered proxy approach and be explicit about its limits: reservation-category fields where the applicant volunteers them, pin-code-level SC/ST population data from the 2011 Census joined to applicant pin-code, and surname-frequency models as a tertiary check, never as a decision input. Report the disagreement gap on each proxy separately, flag the proxy noise, and have legal review the audit methodology before it goes to the regulator. Imperfect signal is still better than "we don't measure it".
counters.weak_html: We can't collect caste so we audit on what we have — gender and age.
counters.why_weak_loses_html: It accepts the data gap as an excuse to not measure the harm. The regulator and the press both already know about pin-code and surname proxies; pretending they don't exist signals either naivete or avoidance, both of which downlevel you.

counters.q: Override rate is going down month over month. Good or bad signal?
counters.strong_html: Genuinely ambiguous — it could mean the model is getting better, or it could mean officers are rubber-stamping because their throughput targets reward speed. I'd disambiguate by sampling 100 non-overridden decisions per officer per month for a blind re-review, and by watching time-to-decision alongside override rate. If override rate drops and time-to-decision drops below the read-and-think floor, that's automation bias, not model improvement, and it's a real risk.
counters.weak_html: Good — it means the model is more trusted now.
counters.why_weak_loses_html: It reads a metric in the most flattering direction without testing the alternative explanation. Every senior interviewer has a story about a vanity metric that turned out to be drift; failing to consider it is a junior pattern.

counters.q: What threshold would you actually set for the kill criteria? Give me a number.
counters.strong_html: For false-decline gap on a protected slice, I'd pre-commit to a pause at any slice running more than 100 basis points above the human-baseline false-decline rate for two consecutive weeks, with an alert at 60 bps. The 100-bp number isn't sacred — it should be set jointly with risk, compliance, and a board sub-committee before launch, and re-anchored each quarter — but the discipline is that the number exists in writing before the breach exists, and the pause is automated, not discretionary.
counters.weak_html: We'd set a threshold based on what we see in production and adjust over time.
counters.why_weak_loses_html: It defers the commitment until after the data arrives, which is exactly how post-hoc thresholds get rationalised upward to avoid a pause. "We'll know it when we see it" is the answer a regulator hears as "we won't pull this feature voluntarily".

traps.move: Leading with accuracy, ROC-AUC, or the eval set
traps.signal_lost_html: Signals you treat AI safety as a leaderboard problem, not an operations problem. Every senior AI interviewer is listening for whether you reach for benchmarks under pressure — if you do, you fail the dimension the question is actually testing.

traps.move: Naming the kill threshold only after the interviewer pushes
traps.signal_lost_html: Pre-commitment is the single clearest signal of regulated-AI seniority. Volunteering the threshold unprompted shows you've shipped under scrutiny; needing to be dragged to it shows you've only read about it.

traps.move: Auditing on aggregate metrics without naming protected slices
traps.signal_lost_html: Aggregate fairness is the textbook way to hide real harm — the whole reason the regulator asked the question is that group-level disparities don't show up in totals. Skipping slices reads as either inexperience or unwillingness to find bad news.

traps.move: Saying the decision log will be "reviewed quarterly" or "handed to compliance"
traps.signal_lost_html: Quarterly-review cadence on a live AI system is the tell — it means you've built the log for the auditor, not for yourself. Senior PMs say they read the override-rate and reason-code dashboards weekly and name which anomaly they'd page on; anything slower than that signals you'd find out about the slice breach from the regulator before you found it yourself.

interview.good_vs_great_html: Hire candidates name the comparator (human underwriters) and the slices. Strong-hire candidates pre-commit, on the call, to the exact memo they would write to the board the day a slice breaches the kill threshold — including who gets notified, what the automated fallback path is, and the wording of the customer communication. Naming the comparator is competence. Naming what you'd do the day you have to pull your own feature is the move that gets "STRONG HIRE — owns the consequence" in the interviewer's notes. It is the difference between someone who designs the measurement programme and someone the company trusts to run it.
interview.meta_skill_html: Turning a regulator-style yes/no question into a pre-committed measurement programme — the durable skill behind every high-stakes AI deployment question, from lending to hiring to medical triage.

under_the_hood.question_html: Everyone tells you to give your AI "memory." But here's the catch — an LLM forgets everything the moment a chat ends. So what is "memory" even referring to?

L326 112"/><path class="ar" d="M472 115 L506 115"/><path class="ar" d="M548 144 C548 196, 74 196, 74 156" stroke-dasharray="4 4"/><text x="300" y="214" text-anchor="middle" class="sub">Every single turn, you re-hand the whole tiffin. It never remembers — you keep re-briefing.</text></svg>

steps.title: So what's actually happening?
steps.body_html: "Memory" is really two boring jobs done well: storing the right notes, and retrieving the relevant ones at the right moment. That second job — fetching the right note at the right time — is exactly what RAG (just a fancy name for "look it up before you answer") does.

steps.title: Why it's not a "smarter model"
steps.body_html: Neither job makes the model more intelligent. They make it better-briefed. A genius with the wrong notes still cooks the wrong dish. Most "the AI is dumb" complaints are actually "the AI was handed bad notes."

steps.title: What this means for you
steps.body_html: When your AI tool feels forgetful or generic, the fix is almost never a bigger model. It's better notes and better retrieval — things you actually control. The hard-feeling problem turns out to be the fixable one.

source.text: Decoded from: "Memory in LLM Agents" survey (arXiv 2024)

the_rep.lite_html: Drop any bill or policy PDF into Claude and ask: "What's the one thing in here most people miss?" Read the answer. That's it.
the_rep.full_html: Drop your own bank's loan FAQ PDF in. Ask: "Find me 3 hidden charges in here." Then compare what it finds against what the website openly states. Screenshot the gap.
the_rep.done: Done looks like: the AI caught at least one charge you'd never noticed. Reply with your screenshot — featured results are shared with permission.

reader_win.quote: Tried it on my company's HR policy. Found a notice-period clause even my manager didn't know about.

toolbox.tool_html: The "explain it back" trick. Before you send any important document — a proposal, a tricky email, a report — paste it into your AI and ask: "Explain this back to me as if you're the person receiving it." Every confusing sentence, every buried ask, shows up in 30 seconds. You're not asking it to write. You're using it as a mirror.
toolbox.try_html: If your AI rephrases your ask differently from what you meant, your reader will too. Caught in 30 seconds.

reality_check.h3: Your "make me 10 images" habit has a water bill
reality_check.body_html: A widely-cited 2024 study (Luccioni et al., Hugging Face + Carnegie Mellon) measured one AI image at roughly the energy of charging a phone halfway — small per image, but it scales fast. Data centres also burn water to stay cool. In India that's not abstract: many sit in already water-stressed cities, on a grid that's still mostly coal. The cost is real but manageable — it's just billed somewhere you don't see, so it's easy to forget it exists.
reality_check.honest_html: The honest framing: this isn't a reason to quit AI, and it's not the apocalypse. It's a reason to use it deliberately — where it earns its cost — and skip the 50 throwaway generations you'll never look at again. Same logic as the Skip List, pointed at your own usage.
reality_check.source: Source: Luccioni, Jernite & Strubell, "Power Hungry Processing" (2024); IEA Electricity 2024 report on data-centre demand. Figures are ranges, not absolutes.

cards.h4: Namma Metro now resolves 40% of commuter complaints via AI
cards.body: Quietly live for months — no launch event, no press release. One of India's largest public-sector AI deployments and almost nobody's covering it.
cards.why_you: this is what real Indian-scale AI deployment looks like — boring, unglamorous, and actually shipped.

cards.h4: NPCI open-sources its UPI fraud-detection model
cards.body: The fraud model behind billions of UPI transactions is now public. Trained on Indian transaction patterns Western datasets never capture.
cards.why_you: a free, production-grade, India-native dataset to learn from. Builders, this is your weekend.

cards.h4: Zoho releases Tamil LLM training data
cards.body: While everyone debates English benchmarks, the hard work of Indian-language AI is happening in Chennai — and they just gave it away.
cards.why_you: if your product touches a Tamil-speaking user, fine-tuning on this beats any English-trained model you'd buy. Free, today.

india_signal.foot: Spotted something India-only worth a Signal?

closer.body_html: We spent two years teaching AI to sound confident. We spent zero teaching it to say "I don't know." So now we have machines that are wrong at the speed of light — and a generation learning to trust the tone instead of checking the fact.

foot.reply_prompt: Reply & tell me — did you do the Rep?

decoder.intro: Section 03 leans into jargon (RAG, evals, citation layer). Here's each in one line — same standard the issue holds itself to.

terms.plain: Look-it-up-before-you-answer. The AI checks a notes file before responding, so its reply cites something instead of guessing from memory.
Test cases for AI. You write down what 'right answer' looks like, then run the AI against your list and count passes.
A small add-on that shows which note the AI used. When a reader asks 'where did this come from?', the system answers honestly.
A specific paragraph the AI pulled from the notes file before answering. The unit of evidence.
Banking, Financial Services, Insurance — short-hand for India's regulated finance industry.
Following the rules a regulator wrote down. Different from doing the right thing in spirit; this is the paperwork version.
